Hey guys, welcome to module three. In this lesson we're going to be talking about segmentation and the confirmation subsequences of the overall framework. So here it is on our map. You've got the beginning of our journey and really what I would consider the two most important subsections of your launch. This is what's really going to set the foundation and ignite your customers journey. You need to set them on that path towards becoming a buyer. And as we learned earlier, just because an email or a sequence doesn't link to a sales page doesn't mean that it's not transactional. It just means that the things we're transacting or in this case coaching are more subtle, more subcutaneous and beneath the surface. But unless we start our campaign with these little microconversions, we're going to have a huge, huge problem trying to get the conversion rates down the road. So with that in mind, what is the segmentation and confirmation coaching? And the first thing should be obvious, it's self-selection. If we expect someone to embark on a journey, they definitely need to enlist. They need to volunteer. They need to raise their hand and say, yes, I am in. And that's why I recommend that you send a segmentation or call out email even if you plan to send your offer and follow up emails to your entire list. Because just the act of getting someone to buy in, to raise their hand, to have that micro commitment is a very important precursor for the later conversion. So don't skip it. But what is it that gets people to raise their hand? And it is really, it comes into excitement about the new outcome. And that's a given. No one's going to come along for a ride if they don't care about the destination. But further, we really need our prospect to get a hint that the ride is actually going to get them there, get them to where they need to go. So it needs to cut through their disillusionment just enough to get them excited. They don't need to know the how right away. That comes later. In fact, it's important at this stage that they don't know about the offer because that's only going to add friction to the excitement. But they do need to feel excited and hopeful enough to move on to the next step. And one of the things that gets people excited actually has nothing to do with you and everything to do with them. That's what I call exclusive empowerment. This is where we're giving them a chance to cash in on their privilege points, on their status. Give them a chance to leverage their status, their power, their achievements, their circumstances, and all those things that are prerequisites for coming along on the ride. So we want to trigger that pride around what they've either accomplished or endured. And when you think about it, this really is a powerful but advanced form of loss aversion. You know, if we're not making the most out of our unique advantages and opportunities, we feel like we're losing the game. So this is really giving them a chance to cash in on that. And even if they're not fully sold on the possibility of the outcome yet, just the fact that they're in a position of exclusive empowerment to pursue it is often enough to get someone on board and excited. So this is a very powerful trigger and it's worth just taking a look at some of the sources of exclusive empowerment. Now, you could start from a place of previous distinctions and accomplishments. So if they're an author of a bestselling book, if they already speak basic Italian, if they already have a successful six-figure online business or possess certain certifications or designations, you know, taking them maybe one-on-one version of your program, all those are very tangible, very concrete examples of what could exclusively empower them to come on this journey. But you could also be more situational and circumstantial. If you're the father of a newborn child, if you work remotely, if you have 10 hours per week to commit to losing weight, for example, if you've recently been laid off from a corporate job, that could be reframed as a positive opportunity. So those are circumstantial. It could even be geographical. We're looking for nurses in Montreal to go to Ottawa, for example. So anything that is situational and circumstantial could also be used as a source of exclusive empowerment. And then finally, probably the most powerful one here is things they've endured. You know, people are very proud of their battle scars sometimes. They really want to add meaning to things they've endured and to pass to things they've overcome. That's probably the thing we feel the most pride around and something that I invite you to try to see if you can tap into. You know, so an example of this would be you've already gone through the withdrawal phase of caffeine addiction or recovered from a cancer diagnosis or rebounded from bankruptcy. So anything that is a trial or tribulation and something they've overcome, there's going to be a lot of pride around that and it's a prime opportunity for you to make that part of the entry ticket of getting into your funnel, going along your buyer's journey. So that is exclusive empowerment. Just to give you an example of how powerful this can be and how it can really shape what a prospect is motivated to do and not motivated to do. A few years ago, when I was still a screenwriter and really looking for what would bring in the income, I was considering law school. You know, and I went through the LSAT, which is kind of the entry exams and I applied kind of begrudgingly. I didn't really want to go to law school. It was kind of a family pressure thing met with, you know, how am I going to make money thing? So I kind of just wrote out that process. I submitted, I applied to a few things and I got a letter at one point. I got a letter from, I think it was the University of Ottawa and I was living in Montreal at the time, which is primarily a French speaking place. And they told me that because I live in Montreal, because I presumably speak French, which I don't, they would be happy to welcome me into a special French version of their program. You know, they were really trying to recruit some bilingual people to come study in that program. And even though I have really no interest in being a lawyer, let alone part taking that program in French, which I don't even speak, or moving to Ottawa, which I would not want to do, no offense to people who live in Ottawa, I was very close to doing it. I had my exclusive empowerment goggles on and it took kind of a final hour withdrawal and a shot of whiskey with a really good friend to talk me out of it. So that's how powerful this trigger of exclusive empowerment can be. And finally, the last thing we're coaching in this sequence is steadfast, friction free commitment to the outcome or what I call opening up the consistency loop so we can close it later in the sales and tighten it in the closing sequence. And because attention moves so fast, you know, we rarely, if ever, stop to add meaning behind the actions we take online. And it's up to you as a conversion coach to add layers of meaning behind that click. You know, you need to take a semi benign action such as an opt in an email sign up and turn that into a steadfast commitment to the outcome. And that's really the role of your confirmation. And it's important to get this commitment at this stage because it's the most friction free environment of your funnel, you know, and that's when we want to get by and we want to get by in that friction free state where your audience, where your prospect is most receptive, their attention is there, they just opted in. And this is what's going to give us a chance to hold them accountable to that outcome later, you know, which I call closing that consistency loop. So you get that buy in in a place of lowered resistance when there's no offer, there's no friction, and then you hold them accountable to it later once it gets recontextualized with your offer. You know, so the same goes with excitement in that outcome, you know, it's a lot easier to get excited when your product isn't in the mix when there's no friction, the stakes are lower. So it's easier to commit to that outcome. It's easier to commit to the excitement around getting on that journey when that friction of the offer isn't there, you know, so this is a risk free transaction of desire before we tie it in to an investment later. So that's really what we're doing. We're getting buy in without the price tag. So this is really kind of the enduring point I want to leave you with as far as what we're coaching, you know, just getting your prospect to say yes to the outcome in that friction free environment and doing so we inherit the right to keep them consistent with it later on in the sale sequence when it is tied to an offer. So that's when we're going to close the consistency loop. That's where we're going to enact our FOMO for them as a coach and really challenge them and hold their feet to the fire and say, I thought you wanted this, you know, you told me that you were ready to commit to this and it's not manipulative. It's really helping them get out of their own way. It's helping them get through that resistance. It's helping them see what they've committed to and holding them accountable to that because prospects do have responsibilities too. You know, our leads have responsibilities and that responsibility that we're holding them to is really staying consistent. So that's the major point here. It's adding that meaning behind the click, you know, that's the confirmation email is probably the most wasted opportunity in any funnel. That's when we really want to reflect back to them, you know, what's probably been going on in their lives that led them to clicking and joining and, you know, really creating that commitment showing them what they're committed to based on that action. That's what is diagnostic, diagnosing the meaning behind the click. And you're going to see some theme plates on how to do that in just a second. But the last thing I kind of want to touch upon is that segmentation can be naturally occurring. You know, if you already have someone on a segmented list, either because they came in through an ad or through partner traffic, then you don't have to send the segmentation call out email that we're going to explore next. However, it's still going to be a good idea to really watch it and go through those exercises so you could see the principles at play, you know, and really, you know, it could be an opportunity to integrate that into your landing page. So regardless of whether or not you're going to be sending out a segmentation call out email, I don't want you to miss out the opportunity to at least absorb those ingredients. And at the very least, at the very least, explore the confirmation email, which is where we're going to be creating that consistency loop, reflecting back and diagnosing what brought them here and reflecting back that meaning so we could hold them accountable to it later. So that is it. Once again, confirmation email, most under leveraged in online marketing. It's the one that doesn't get the love it deserves. It's going to be your most highly open email. Generally, you know, speaking, more people are going to open that than anything else. And it's your best opportunity to really reflect back that meaning, you know, so we're going to learn how to do that in detail when we do the walkthroughs of the what this says about you deamplate. This is usually my starting point when optimizing campaigns for clients and it's crazy how much of an impact a well-crafted confirmation email can make. So on that note, I'm going to duck out. It's time to start putting in the work. So if you haven't already done so, go ahead, download the workbook for this module and meet me in the next section where we're going to do a walkthrough on that and explore the deamplate. So I will see you in the next video.
